request: We need an app for staff to check attendees in at the venue entrance on the event day.

problem.value: Control physical entry, verify each attendee is legitimate/registered, prevent duplicate or fraudulent entries, and know who is actually on-site.
problem.evidence: "check attendees in at the venue entrance on the event day"

success_metrics.value: Fast throughput at the door, no duplicate/fraudulent entries, accurate real-time headcount.
success_metrics.evidence: implied by 'check attendees in at the venue entrance'

actors.value: Staff (checker) explicit; Attendees implied; likely also an event organizer/admin needing oversight and possibly security/supervisor roles for exceptions.

business_objects.value: Attendee/Registration, Ticket or badge/QR code, Event (single day), Check-in record.

workflow.value: Attendee arrives, staff verifies identity/ticket, system marks as checked-in, attendee admitted. Single-day event; multi-entrance and re-entry rules unknown.
workflow.evidence: "check attendees in at the venue entrance on the event day"

permissions.value: Staff need check-in rights; organizer/admin likely needs oversight/override rights; unclear if multiple staff tiers exist (e.g. VIP line vs general).

constraints.value: Hard deadline is the event day itself with no slippage possible; venue connectivity (wifi/cellular) at the entrance is uncertain and directly affects architecture.

questions.q: Where does the list of valid attendees come from - an existing registration/ticketing system to sync from, a spreadsheet import, or manual entry on the day?
questions.why: high impact, near-empty: decides whether this is a standalone tool or must integrate with/import from a registration system, and whether walk-in registration must be built.

questions.q: Will the entrance have reliable internet connectivity, or must the app work offline and sync later?
questions.why: high impact, inferred only: offline-capable check-in is a fundamentally different technical build (local storage, conflict resolution, sync) than an always-online app.

questions.q: What identifies an attendee at the door - a QR/barcode ticket, a name lookup, an ID check, or some combination - and can one ticket be used by multiple people or scanned twice?
questions.why: high impact, empty: defines the verification method and duplicate/fraud rules, which drive both the UI and the core validation logic.

questions.q: Are there multiple entrances or staff devices operating simultaneously, and if so, does a check-in on one device need to instantly block re-entry attempts on another?
questions.why: medium-high impact if true: concurrent check-in across devices requires real-time sync/locking, a very different build from a single-device kiosk.

questions.q: Does anyone need a live headcount/dashboard during the event, or a report afterward (e.g. who attended, no-shows)?
questions.why: medium impact, empty: clarifies whether a simple check-in log suffices or a live dashboard/export is required.

summary.objective: Give venue staff a way to verify and check attendees in at the entrance on the day of the event, ensuring only legitimate registrants are admitted and an accurate record of who is on-site is kept.
summary.scope: Likely a lightweight mobile/tablet app for staff that looks up or scans an attendee's ticket/registration, validates it against the guest list, marks them as checked-in, and flags duplicates or invalid entries. May need offline capability, multi-device sync across entrances, and a basic live count or post-event attendance report.
assumptions: The event is single-day with attendees pre-registered elsewhere (ticketing/registration system) rather than registered live.
Staff will use handheld devices (phone/tablet) at the entrance rather than fixed kiosks.
Some form of ticket identifier (QR code, barcode, or name) exists to look up each attendee.
An organizer/admin role exists for oversight beyond front-line staff.
summary.blind_spot: Venue connectivity and multi-device/multi-entrance concurrency: if the network drops or multiple staff scan simultaneously, the app must handle offline operation and prevent the same ticket being double-used across devices - this changes the technical architecture far more than the check-in screen itself.

problem.value: Enable fast, reliable verification of who is authorized to enter the venue on event day, avoiding fraud/duplicate entry and bottlenecks at the door.
problem.evidence: "staff to check attendees in at the venue entrance on the event day" — the ask is the mechanism, not the underlying goal (access control + flow management).

current_process.value: Likely a paper list, spreadsheet, or generic ticketing app today; no dedicated check-in tool mentioned.
current_process.evidence: No as-is process described; inferred from the fact a new app is being requested for a task commonly done manually.

success_metrics.value: Likely: check-in speed per attendee, zero duplicate/fraudulent entries, accurate real-time headcount.
success_metrics.evidence: Not stated; inferred from the nature of entrance check-in tools.

actors.value: Staff perform the check-in at the entrance. Roles beyond "staff" (e.g. event manager/admin managing the guest list, supervisor overseeing multiple entrances) are not specified.
actors.evidence: "staff to check attendees in" is explicit; no mention of admin/manager roles.

business_objects.value: Likely: Attendee/Guest, Ticket/Registration, Event, Check-in record. Source of the attendee list is unclear.
business_objects.evidence: Not detailed in request; inferred from domain (event check-in).

business_rules.value: Unknown: verification method (QR/ticket scan, name lookup, ID check), duplicate-entry prevention, capacity limits, plus-ones, walk-ins.
business_rules.evidence: Nothing said about how identity/eligibility is verified — this is the core mechanic of the whole app.

workflow.value: Unknown: single-entrance vs multi-entrance sync, online vs offline mode, check-in-only vs check-in/check-out lifecycle.
workflow.evidence: Not addressed; event-day tools commonly need multi-device sync and offline fallback (venue connectivity is a classic trap).

integrations.value: Unknown: is there a pre-existing registration/ticketing system to sync the guest list from, and in which direction/frequency?
integrations.evidence: No mention of a source system; "check attendees in" implies a list must already exist somewhere.

permissions.value: Unknown: do all staff have equal rights, or are there tiers (check-in only vs. edit guest list vs. view reports)?
permissions.evidence: Only "staff" mentioned as a single undifferentiated role.

config_vs_custom.value: Unknown whether this is a one-off single-event tool or a reusable platform for multiple recurring events/clients.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Not stated; affects whether rules/labels should be hardcoded or configurable.

constraints.value: Fixed, non-negotiable deadline (event day) and likely uncertain venue network connectivity — no room for a failed rollout on the day itself.
constraints.evidence: "on the event day" implies a hard, single-shot deadline; venue entrances often have poor connectivity.

edge_cases.value: Unknown handling of: attendee not on list, lost/duplicate ticket, plus-ones, VIP fast-track, already-checked-in re-scan.

reporting.value: Likely a simple live headcount/attendance export; no compliance or audit obligation mentioned.
reporting.evidence: No regulatory or audit language in the request — kept at baseline low impact.

risks.value: Single-day, single-shot event: device failure, connectivity loss, or list-sync errors on the day cannot be patched after the fact.
risks.evidence: Inferred from "event day" being a hard, unrepeatable deadline.

questions.q: How is an attendee verified at the door — a scannable ticket/QR code, a name lookup against a guest list, ID check, or a combination?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: this defines the entire check-in mechanism (scanning hardware, lookup logic, fraud prevention) and is currently completely unstated — highest-impact blind spot.

questions.q: Where does the attendee/guest list come from — is there an existing registration or ticketing system to sync with, and does it need to update in real time or is a pre-loaded static list enough?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: determines whether this is a standalone app or must integrate with another system; also drives idempotency (avoiding duplicate entries) and data completeness.

questions.q: Will the venue have reliable network connectivity, or must the app work fully offline at the entrance and sync check-ins afterward?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: offline-capable vs. always-online is a fundamentally different build (local storage, conflict resolution, retry logic) and connectivity at venues is a classic failure point.

questions.q: Will there be multiple entrances/devices checking in against the same list simultaneously, and do all staff have equal permissions, or are some restricted to scanning only?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: multi-device concurrency changes sync/idempotency requirements, and undifferentiated 'staff' may hide a need for tiered access (e.g. supervisor override for problem cases).

questions.q: What should happen when someone isn't on the list, has a duplicate/lost ticket, or has already been checked in — reject, flag for manual override, or allow with a warning?
questions.why: uncertainty × impact: these are the highest-friction moments at a live event door and directly shape UX and staff escalation paths, yet nothing is specified.

summary.objective: Give venue staff a reliable, fast way to verify and record attendee entry on the day of the event, preventing unauthorized or duplicate entry while keeping the line moving.
summary.scope: Likely a mobile/tablet app for staff to scan or look up attendees against a guest list, mark them as checked-in, and surface a live status (checked-in/not found/already used). Probably includes a simple attendance view/export for organizers.
assumptions: No dedicated check-in tool exists today; this replaces a manual/paper or generic process.
Success is measured by check-in speed, accuracy (no duplicates/fraud), and a reliable headcount.
This is likely a single-event, time-boxed deadline rather than a recurring configurable platform (to be confirmed).
Reporting needs are basic (attendance count/export), with no compliance/audit obligation mentioned.
summary.blind_spot: The request assumes 'check attendees in' is a simple lookup-and-tap action, but it hides the real build drivers: the verification method (ticket/QR vs. name vs. ID), whether the guest list is synced from another system, whether the app must work offline at a potentially poor-connectivity venue, and how staff handle the inevitable edge cases (not on list, duplicate scan, already checked in) live at the door — these determine most of the actual engineering effort, not the 'check-in' action itself.

problem.value: Replace manual/paper attendee verification at the entrance with a fast, reliable digital check-in to reduce queues, prevent fraud (duplicate/invalid entries), and give organizers visibility into arrivals.
problem.evidence: Explicit request states the need; the underlying pain (speed, fraud, queue control) is inferred.

current_process.evidence: Not stated whether they currently use paper lists, spreadsheets, a ticketing tool, or nothing at all.

success_metrics.evidence: Not stated — likely candidates are check-in speed, reduced queue time, accurate attendance count, no duplicate/fraudulent entries.

actors.value: Staff members operate the app at the entrance to check in attendees; an organizer/admin likely manages the attendee list and views results; attendees are the subject, not app users.
actors.evidence: Explicit: 'staff to check attendees in'. Admin/organizer role is inferred, not stated.

business_objects.value: Attendee/Registration (name, ticket reference), Event, CheckIn record (timestamp, staff member, entrance/gate), possibly Ticket type (VIP/general).
business_objects.evidence: Inferred from typical event check-in domain; no field list or data source given.

business_rules.evidence: Not specified: how is an attendee validated (QR code, name search, ID)? What happens on duplicate check-in, invalid ticket, or capacity limits by ticket tier?

workflow.value: Likely: attendee arrives, staff verifies identity/ticket, marks checked-in, timestamp recorded. Possible variants: multiple entrances, re-entry/checkout, VIP fast lane.
workflow.evidence: Baseline event-industry flow inferred; not confirmed by client, no mention of re-entry or multiple gates.

integrations.evidence: Not stated where the attendee/registration list comes from — an existing ticketing/registration system, a spreadsheet import, or manual entry. This determines most of the architecture.

permissions.value: Staff likely restricted to check-in actions only; an organizer/admin role likely needed to view/edit the attendee list and see overall results.
permissions.evidence: Inferred from 'staff' being named as sole explicit actor; no admin role or access levels stated.

config_vs_custom.evidence: No indication this is a multi-client configurable platform vs a single-event app; not enough context to activate this slot meaningfully.

constraints.value: Hard deadline implied ('event day') — the app must be ready and reliable for a fixed date; likely needs offline capability at the venue since connectivity at entrances can be unreliable.
constraints.evidence: Inferred from 'on the event day' phrasing; no explicit date, venue connectivity, or device constraints given.

edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed: lost/forgotten ticket, duplicate check-in attempt, attendee not on list, network outage at venue, walk-ins, plus-ones/group tickets.

reporting.evidence: No compliance/audit driver named yet; likely just a live attendance count and export, not a regulatory requirement.

risks.value: Main risk: connectivity failure or slow lookup causing entrance bottlenecks on the day itself, with no fallback — a single-day, no-retry event makes reliability critical.
risks.evidence: Inferred from the one-shot, time-critical nature of 'event day' check-in; not explicitly discussed by client.

questions.q: Where does the attendee/registration list come from — an existing ticketing or registration system, a spreadsheet import, or manual entry into this app?
questions.why: High impact, empty: this decides the entire architecture (sync/import mechanism, data freshness, duplicate handling) and whether we're building a source of truth or just a front-end to one.

questions.q: How should staff validate an attendee — scanning a QR/barcode ticket, searching by name, or checking an ID? And what should happen on a duplicate check-in or an attendee not found on the list?
questions.why: High impact, empty: this defines the core interaction and the exception paths (duplicate/fraud prevention), which is the crux of what 'check-in' even means here.

questions.q: Will the venue have reliable internet/wifi, or does the app need to work offline (with later sync) at the entrance?
questions.why: High impact, inferred only: offline-first vs online-only is a fundamentally different build, and getting it wrong risks the app failing exactly when it matters most — the event day itself.

questions.q: Besides check-in staff, does anyone need an admin/organizer view — to upload the attendee list, monitor arrivals live, or export results after the event?
questions.why: High impact, inferred: determines whether this is a single-role scanning tool or a two-sided system with list management and live reporting, which changes scope significantly.

questions.q: Are there multiple entrances/staff devices working simultaneously, and could the same attendee be scanned at two entrances at once?
questions.why: Medium impact but directly tied to the business_rules gap: concurrent check-in across devices is a classic blind spot that changes whether we need real-time sync vs. simple local state.

summary.objective: Give venue staff a fast, reliable way to verify and check in attendees at the entrance on event day, replacing manual list-checking and reducing queues and entry fraud.
summary.scope: Likely scope: a staff-facing app (mobile/tablet) to look up or scan an attendee's ticket/registration, mark them checked-in with a timestamp, and prevent duplicate entries; probably paired with an admin capability to load the attendee list beforehand and view check-in results during/after the event.
assumptions: There is an underlying attendee/registration list to check against (source not yet identified).
Staff are the only ones actively using the app on event day; an organizer/admin role exists for setup and monitoring.
The event is single-day with a hard deadline, so reliability and possibly offline capability matter more than long-term configurability.
Basic check-in workflow: verify → mark checked-in → timestamp, with no re-entry/checkout logic assumed yet.
summary.blind_spot: The client asked for a check-in app but didn't say what the attendees are being checked against (ticketing system? spreadsheet? nothing yet) or how validation happens (QR scan vs. name lookup vs. ID) — and whether the venue will even have connectivity. These decide whether this is a lightweight front-end to an existing system or a full registration + check-in build, and whether it must work offline. Building the wrong one risks a broken entrance experience on the one day there's no room for error.
